data.title: Hilton Grand Vacations: The Upward Trip Is Already A Bit Stretched
data.description:
It has been three months since my previous coverage of Hilton Grand Vacations, Inc. (HGV). Its value has already risen by $5.41, or 14%, despite my hold rating. Somehow this is understandable considering its robust performance during the quarter and the bullish trend of the US stock market. However, valuation still advises some caution as the stock appears fully priced. Technicals adhere to it as selling pressures emerge even if the overall momentum remains bullish.

HGV Q4 2025: Growth Overstayed, Efficiency Fueled Margins

Discretionary spending is still constrained by macroeconomic headwinds. And even the hospitality industry is not entirely shielded from the impact. Although travel spending has remained resilient in the past year, we saw how consumer/tourist behavior shifted to fit their budget into the current environment. Despite this, the preference and demand for luxury services appear to be favorable for timeshares like Hilton Grand Vacations, Inc. You can see it in its most recent performance.

In Q4 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $1.33B, up by 3.8% YoY from $1.28B. This served as a decent comeback from its weaker YoY performance in Q3 2025. Sales of VOIs remained the primary revenue component and growth driver as they comprised 37% of the total and rose by 9% YoY. This means that the value of all the timeshares sold in Q4 increased. If we look at the actual revenue segment, real estate sales and financing increased YoY. And I think the recent policy easing cycle also supported it since lower borrowing costs attracted new property members/owners. Its lower pricing strategies in my previous coverage also worked as tour flow increased. As you can see, the VPG decreased, which means that HGV made lower average sales per customer who attended a sales presentation or tour. Yet volume offset it considering the increase in contract sales from $837M to $852M.

In addition, HGV improved its efficiency if we look at how the increase in operating expenses remained manageable. Also, the cost of VOI sales decreased. As a result, the operating margin improved to 12.5% versus 10.4% YoY.

Key Considerations

Travel spending may have remained resilient in the past year, but one must still be wary of stubborn inflation. For instance, the US inflation of 2.4% is still higher than the Fed’s 2.0% target, and positive inflation means prices are increasing. New tariffs may exacerbate it since they raise the production costs of domestic businesses, which can lead to higher domestic prices.

The tensions in the Middle East can also impact travel in some ways. Why? Oil prices have skyrocketed again recently. In fact, Brent and WTI are now at their 13-month high of $81.99 per barrel and $74.95 per barrel. This will push the prices of refined oil products up, which means that fuel may become more expensive. With that, transportation expenses may increase, which may affect the travel budget of many domestic and international tourists. And because oil is crucial in running the economy, the overall price level may be affected.

Liquidity is also a consideration, although this is not an immediate risk. As a timeshare business, HGV is capital-intensive. So, it’s not surprising to see its high borrowings. Cash may be ample at $239 but remains relatively lower than the total borrowings of $7.02B. With that, the Net Debt/EBITDA is 8.4x, which means that HGV needs 8.4 years to repay the whole amount at once even if it doesn’t have to.

Of course, its EBITDA of $834M won’t be exhausted to repay the borrowings. Suppose only half of it is used, the ratio or the number of years will double to 16-17 years. The consolation is that HGV’s maturity profile of its debt is still decent, with most not maturing within five years.

Potential Risk Mitigants

Despite these challenges, there are still some aspects that can mitigate their impact.

One aspect is the changes in the behavior of many ultra-rich and/or high-net-worth individuals. The information presented by The Economist shows that as liquidity injected into the economy decreased following the staggering inflation in 2022-2024, rich individuals shifted their preference for luxury assets. From owning luxury wines, paintings, and mansions, the rate of ownership has decreased since 2023. These items appeared less status-defining and less exclusive than they used to be. To make it simple, the value drivers of luxury goods and services have changed in recent years. No, wealthy individuals are spending more on experiences and services. These include exclusive hotels, resort and beach properties, and Michelin-star dining experiences. With that, timeshares stand to benefit from these trends. After all, it targets individuals from opulent income classes.

Another factor to help it is the recent policy easing cycle. This can partially offset the impact of inflation since lower borrowing costs can support production and consumption. This can also reduce the interest rates on HGV’s variable-rate borrowings, which can help improve its liquidity.

Lastly, its strategic business model mix may help balance its growth with liquidity. Typically, timeshares can be cost-sensitive and capital-intensive. That is why HGV’s primary revenue component is its real estate sales and financing. But with its fee-based model, it can expand its brand popularity across the globe without bleeding billions. Aside from that, it enjoys extra revenue streams from fees like resort and club management, rental, and cost reimbursements. Additionally, timeshares have a fixed/secure revenue stream because owners have to pay continuously. Aside from the upfront purchase price, they have to pay annual maintenance fees, special assessments for renovations and upgrades, and sometimes exchange transaction fees if owners trade their owned week. Timeshare ownership is also typically contract-based, like 20 years, while contracts are perpetual.

Valuation

HGV has increased in recent months, so I decided to reassess its valuation using various price ratios. Using P/E, the stock is already trading at 48.44x its EPS versus the five-year average of 38.55x. If we use it to derive its fair value, the TP or target price will be $34.31. Now, one may argue that price ratios only show how cheap or expensive it is relative to previous years. But the thing is, the P/E of almost 50x speaks a lot about its value. Without 2024, the current P/E is already the highest since 2021.

The same applies to P/B, which shows that it trades at 2.78x its book value versus the average of 2.40x. This leads to a TP of $37.20. Meanwhile, P/CPS or cash per share and P/FCF or free cash flow suggest higher TPs of $41.99 and $40.18, respectively. However, they show that the stock is already fully priced. With that, more upside is not that justifiable.

Technicals

HGV’s technicals are still generally bullish as the price still increases with higher highs and lows. However, the huge selling candle that appeared three days ago signaled the sellers’ attempt to drag the price down. The day before that, we already saw market indecision. And for two consecutive days, the stock price has been below the 50-day SMA, which means that the current momentum has weakened relative to the average in the past 50 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA is still trending upwards, but we can see its tendency to flatten, which suggests caution among investors.

Its MACD and RSI show the same thing. The MACD line is decreasing below the signal line amid the decreasing buying volume and increasing selling volume. Meanwhile, the histogram is already within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. Lastly, RSI is decreasing, showing weakening momentum. But if it leads to overselling, it may open new buying opportunities.

Conclusion

Hilton Grand Vacations, Inc. remains strong with its large customer base, supported by its pricing strategies and operational efficiencies. However, it must watch out for potential market headwinds that can affect its performance in H1 2026. Its valuation is also not exactly attractive and shows some downside risks. Technicals adhere to it, as weakening momentum warrants some caution among investors. I’m reiterating my hold rating on this stock.

data.title: Floor & Decor: Still Too Early To Upgrade This To Buy
data.description:
Investment action

I had a hold rating for Floor & Decor (FND) previously, as I was still worried about the macro headwinds and rather limited unit growth, albeit I liked the potential for a V-shaped recovery. I am keeping my neutral view for now. Demand got worse, but margin was a positive surprise (like-for-like gross margin). I do not think the setup is weak enough to turn bearish (especially with rates coming down), but I also do not think there is enough evidence yet to justify a bullish upgrade.

4Q25 earnings review

This was a soft Q4 for FND. Revenue was up a mere 2% y/y, which was really helped by new stores while the core store base weakened. Comparable store sales fell 4.8%, implying that underlying demand remains weak. But margins and profits were weaker. Gross margin was flat y/y at 43.5%, while selling, general, and administrative expenses as a percentage of sales went up to 38.9% from 38.1% last year. This offset the 2% topline growth, resulting in operating income falling by 12.3% to $51.9 million, and the operating margin declined to 4.6%. Ultimately, net income fell 17.2% to $39.3 million, while diluted EPS fell 18.2% to $0.36.

Reasons for my neutral view

I think it's obvious that the biggest negative for FND this quarter is the core sales trend. Back in Q3, comparable store sales were down 1.2%, and that has worsened to -4.8%, which is a huge step down. That wasn’t even the worst. The monthly cadence worsened through the quarter, from down 1.5% in October to down 6.1% in November and down 6.7% in December. The right number to anchor against for Q1 2026 is really 6.7%, and not 4.8%. Of that 4.8% decline this quarter, transactions declined 4.2%, and the average ticket fell 0.6%. All these numbers are not noise. They show FND is still struggling with demand.

Management did mention January improved to positive comparable sales, but I caution investors not to extrapolate that because Winter Storm Fern impacted more than half the store base and the Baltimore distribution center. Management literally said that disruption resulted in lost sales and significant pressure on quarter-to-date comps. They even said those lost sales are not expected to be fully recovered within Q1. Bloomberg’s alternative data also paints the same picture: that Q1 will see negative comps.

Then, if you consider that FND does not really have a second growth lever to offset the weak comps right now, it is just hard to imagine growth acceleration. Specifically, management only plans to open 20 warehouse-format stores in 2026 (same as in 2025). That implies unit growth is still there, but it is not accelerating. And if comps are negative and store openings are only running at the same pace as last year, the overall growth algorithm just cannot be that strong. Lower CapEx per store is helpful for per-store unit economics, but it does not change the fact that revenue growth will stay limited. In other words, until organic housing-derived demand improves and comps recover, I don’t see a path for FND’s top-line growth to meaningfully reaccelerate.

That said, I must give credit to management for managing the margins. While headline gross margin was flat, the like-for-like margin actually went up (due to better product margin) but was offset by headwinds tied to the Seattle and Baltimore distribution center expansion. Pair that with most of FND’s store fleet that can still flex labor with demand, and you can imagine FY2026 margin to be better. Management also expects previous G&A initiatives to annualize into 2026. Of course, this doesn’t solve the demand problem, but it does help protect the earnings.

Valuation

Previously, my valuation angle was that if one were to invest in FND at 29x NTM PE, why not invest in Microsoft (MSFT) given MSFT is more resilient and has a better growth outlook? I still think that framework makes sense, just that the narrative for MSFT today is murkier because of the threat from AI. MSFT now trades at 22x NTM PE, while FND trades at 31x. By my previous logic, there is just no reason to be long FND. But I do see a case for FND to generate better returns in the near term. If the V-shaped recovery comes, we could see significant earnings growth within the near term, and that could make the current 31x NTM PE look cheap. Rates are coming down, and this is a big catalyst for demand if the trend continues.

In numbers, assuming that the high-teens growth gets pulled forward, say 18% starting from FY25 ($1.92), FY27 adj. EPS would be ~$2.7, which means FND would be trading at 24x FY27 numbers by the end of this year. That is not bad, and with momentum riding, the market could easily value FND at a higher multiple than 24x.

The problem is timing. In the call, management made it clear that housing affordability and macro uncertainty are still weighing on larger discretionary purchases. They also clearly said that the early Q1 weather disruption makes it harder to judge the true underlying demand trend until later in the spring. This means we are unlikely to get incremental positive news flow until Q1 2026 results. From now till then, I think the stock is going to stay range-bound.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating on FND. My view remains that this is a business with decent recovery upside, but the timing is still the issue. Comps remain weak, store growth is not accelerating, and management made it clear near-term demand likely remains weak because of macro pressure and the Q1 weather disruption. All of these make it hard to build a strong case for a rerating today.

data.title: Barclays: The Market Overheated, But The Bull Case Now Offers Strong Returns
data.description:
Introduction

I shall begin this note by reminding readers that banks are highly leveraged entities that are sensitive to changes in underlying economic conditions that are outside their control. As a result, when things unexpectedly come along (as they always do) that cause investors to feel worried about the economic outlook, bank share prices often get smashed. Conversely, when fears subside and investors become complacent about risk, bank share prices can run hard. Leverage is two-directional.

Movements in the Barclays PLC (BCS) share price over the last twelve months demonstrate the above nicely. From here on, I will refer to Barclays as BARC, which is the code that the company trades under on the London Stock Exchange. Going back to the first round of US tariff madness in April 2025, BARC hit a low of 223.75p. Had you invested at that low point, by early February 2026, when BARC peaked at 506.40p, you'd have been sitting on a gain of 126%. In addition, you'd have picked up a nice little 1.3% dividend yield. Unfortunately, if you hadn't sold any of your holdings into the early February 2026 peak, with BARC now trading back down at ~430p, you're probably presently thinking that you got a little too greedy.

When things are going well for banks, I often see research notes that seem to suggest that banks are steady, predictable money-making machines. For UK banks in particular, of late I've noticed a lot of commentary about the supposedly locked-in massive future profits from structural hedge books (which are essentially an interest rate risk management tool). Readers of my prior work on BARC will be aware that I have a far less bullish opinion regarding UK banking structural hedge books. I think that the swings in the BARC share price nicely demonstrate the fallacy of the structural hedge bull case - if the touted huge structural hedge profits were indeed locked in, why would the BARC share price move around so violently? Surely the buffer of locked-in profits would provide share price stability? The answer to that conundrum is that investors realize that a bank's future sustainable earnings profile is dependent upon a wide range of variables, the collective impact of which dwarfs anything that is going on in the structural hedge books.

I don't make this point in order to stir up a debate about BARC's structural hedge book, and I would not disagree with any analysis that referenced the benefit that BARC has and will continue to receive from this risk management tool. Instead, I use it as a reminder that despite the mirage of earnings stability that can come from forecasting future structural hedge income or, similarly, many more years of low bad debt charges, banking earnings are a very long way from being a reliable income stream. For value investors like me, this is good news from an investment perspective. Markets will inevitably push bank share prices too low when fear is high and boost them up to unsustainable levels when complacency and/or greed is rife.

In this note I’ll explain why I believe that investors were over-optimistic when recently buying BARC at above 500p. I’ll also set out a bull case for the bank and provide some pushback on that bull case in order to help readers form their own view. To be clear, at 500p, I saw BARC as a Hold at best. But with the share price now hovering down at around 430p, I’m comfortable retaining a Buy rating. My BARC notes usually dig into operational detail, including divisional-level analysis, but on this occasion, I’m going to keep things quite high level.

FY25 Beats Consensus

BARC delivered a solid FY25 result, coming in ahead of the most recently published (January 30, 2026) consensus numbers. As shown in Exhibit 1, pre-tax profit printed 1.5% ahead of consensus. However, 47% of the overall £132m beat can be attributed to lower credit impairment (which I also refer to as ‘bad debt’) charges, which is unlikely to be a sustainable outcome.

Exhibit 1:

The consensus beat, combined with pre-tax profit of £9,139m, up 13% on FY24, and RoTE increasing to 11.3% from 10.5% in FY24, suggests to me that the FY25 result itself has not been a source of disappointment for the market. I also note that FY25 came in ahead or in line with each item that management had given guidance on (as shown in Exhibit 2).

Exhibit 2:

Management had flagged last year that a new set of forward-looking multi-year targets would be given at the FY25 presentation. Exhibit 3 sets out a summary of the new FY26E and FY28E targets. BARC is targeting compound annual income growth of greater than 5% over 2025-2028. On the cost front, BARC has a target to achieve total gross efficiency savings of ~£2bn in 2026-2028. The new financial targets show no sign of management expecting a downturn in BARC’s operational performance.

Exhibit 3:

So, with BARC having delivered FY25 numbers that were ahead of both consensus and management guidance and setting out reasonably ambitious new financial targets, we still haven’t identified a potential source of the recent fall in BARC’s share price that was apparent before the broader equity market weakness associated with the US-Iran conflict. Let’s dig a bit deeper.

BARC Was Clearly Overheated In Early February 2026

In Exhibit 4 below, I compare BARC consensus earnings for FY26E and FY27E taken as of October 10, 2025, with BARC consensus earnings taken as of January 30, 2026.

Exhibit 4:

The analysis above shows that 1-2 year forward consensus pre-tax profit had increased by 3.4% to 4.9% between mid-October 2025 and late January 2026. BARC’s share price moved upward from 372.5p to 486.2p, a gain of 30.5%, over that same period. Given that the change in consensus earnings was 3.4% to 4.9%, the rapid increase in BARC’s share price implies a very large expansion in the stock’s earnings multiple. Back in late January and early February 2026, when BARC nudged up over the 500p mark, I was scratching my head as to why BARC’s earnings multiple should have expanded so rapidly.

In retrospect, it is now clear that the market simply got ahead of itself. Further, at its early February 2026 peak price of 506.4p, BARC was trading at 1.13x forecast FY26E TNAV (tangible net asset value). A one-year forward price-to-TNAV multiple of 1.13 struck me as slightly overstretched given that the FY26E consensus RoTE (return on tangible equity) of 12.7% factors in significant TNAV growth and credit impairment charges that I believe to be several hundred million pounds below normalized levels.

The BARC Bull Case

As shown in Exhibit 4 above, management has put out a bullish FY28 target for RoTE of at least 14%. I remind readers that this is for a bank that just a few years ago looked like it was struggling to achieve what was then deemed to be an ambitious 10% RoTE target - quite a leap forward. Part of the improvement in BARC’s profitability has come from sensible strategy and good execution. External factors, notably a prolonged upward shift in interest rates and a continuation of benign bad debt charges, have provided tailwinds that management ought not to be given much credit for. On balance, I think BARC has won over the market, and the consensus view is that management will be able to deliver on their new targets. The bull case that follows is framed by that perspective.

Consensus is forecasting TNAV per share of 496p at FY27E, up from reported TNAV per share of 409p at FY25. If the market starts to believe that BARC’s RoTE target of >14% is achievable on a sustainable basis, I believe that a P:TNAV multiple of 1.2x would be easily justifiable (and maybe closer to 1.4x). On that basis, we are looking at potential share price gains of ~38% over the next two years. In addition, we have potential dividend returns of ~2.7% pa. The total return under this scenario would be close to 20% pa.

The FY28E RoTE > 14% target is backed up by both persuasive revenue and cost arguments. BARC is aiming to deliver loan book growth of over 5% pa in Barclays UK, the group’s UK retail and business banking division. On the net interest income front, BARC highlights the structural hedge income tailwind.

I have consistently underestimated earnings in the Global Markets segment of BARC’s Investment Banking division. BARC delivered further income growth from this revenue stream in FY25 (up 15% on FY24). I am not convinced that the Investment Banking division can reach the 13% RoTE level that BARC is allowing for within its overall >14% RoTE group target. But relative to my prior views regarding normalised Investment Banking earnings, I see the prospect of further improvement from this division as achievable.

Turning to costs, BARC has demonstrated that it can successfully take costs out of the business while at the same time improving its revenue streams. Over the period 2026 to 2028, BARC is planning to achieve gross efficiency savings of some £2bn. This will be achieved by investing in improving the bank’s technology infrastructure, standardising data platforms, and embedding AI for productivity and product construction and delivery. An associated goal is that BARC comes out of 2028 as a simpler, more digitised bank than it is today. Concrete examples of simplification are a) BARC is aiming to reduce the number of trading book subledgers that it runs from 11 to 3, b) BARC plans to more than halve the current 80+ applications used to manage risk in the corporate and investment banking divisions.

On the capital front, BARC is starting its 2026 to 2028 plan from a position of strength. This is highlighted by a CET1 ratio of 14.3%, a planned £2bn dividend, and the announcement of a £1bn buyback alongside the FY25 result. In total, BARC plans to return over £15bn of capital over the 2026 to 2028 period.

Push-back On The Bull Case

If you are going to sign on to the BARC bull case, it’s important to consider its points of vulnerability, along with other downside risk factors that it overlooks. For me, I don’t see the below points as overly weighty in regard to my bullish stance. But I include this discussion to offer balance, and to allow readers to make their own risk assessments. In a way, the bull case sounds almost too easy. In reality, there are headwinds and potential minefields that must be dealt with.

My analysis suggests that management and sell-side consensus is underestimating normalised bad debt charges for BARC. In my estimates, this factor has the potential to reduce the expected FY28E RoTE by around 0.8%. Note here, that I am only talking about a return to normalised (or ‘through-the-cycle’) bad debt charges.

Going a step further on bad debts, the bull case is clearly not contemplating the risk of a severe credit cycle. Whilst at present there are few alarming warning signs on credit quality, we don’t have to work our imagination too hard to come up with downside scenarios. Two topical ones are a) negative AI related impacts on businesses and white-collar workers that trigger loan defaults, and b) a blowup in private credit markets.

On the latter point, we are already seeing some signs of stress in private credit – if this is just the start of something much bigger, then I would expect BARC and many other large institutional banks to incur significant losses from their exposures to players in the private credit market. Readers interested in my broader views on private credit, and specifically why I believe this so called ‘asset class’ is not suitable for retail investors, may wish to read my recent note on Franklin Resources (BEN).

On the income front, AI-related white-collar worker job losses may cause a significant slowdown in retail credit demand (notably mortgages and credit cards). If central banks find themselves needing to lower interest rates in order to combat such an AI-related surge in unemployment, we will likely see downward pressure on net interest income as net interest margins shrink.

In regard to capital distributions, it made absolute sense for BARC to pursue aggressive share buybacks when its share price was below TNAV per share. But with the share price above TNAV per share, I see this as an increasingly unfavourable strategy. I would prefer the bank to now return capital to shareholders primarily in the form of dividends, alongside a dividend reinvestment plan that would serve investor appetite for reinvestment. However, I accept that there are arguments against my view – notably around tax efficiency and the somewhat unconvincing argument that buybacks are more flexible than dividends (which boards tend not to like to reduce).

Closing Remarks & Rating

BARC has executed very well over recent years, and the management team and board are to be commended on this. However, I believe that by late January 2026, the market had become too optimistic about the bank’s medium-term outlook, and had pushed the BARC share price to a level where I could no longer justify a Buy rating.

With BARC falling back from its highs and trading at ~430p, I am once again comfortable with a Buy rating. BARC is trading at a premium of ~5% to its FY25 TNAV per share of 409p. After considering the bull case arguments set out above, I expect the BARC share price to reward holders due to strong growth in TNAV and further expansion in BARC’s Price:TNAV multiple.

data.title: EPR Properties: The Ride Won't Last Forever, But I'm Holding (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I'm always quite sentimental when I cover EPR Properties (EPR). It was the first business I wrote about here at Seeking Alpha. I've covered it several times since then, and in general, I've been very bullish on this unique REIT, but I'm very happy with my initial coverage. Since then (May 2024), EPR's stock price increased by over 37% and delivered nearly 56% total return thanks to EPR's very attractive (in my taste) dividends.

The last time I covered it was in the beginning of December 2025. Since then, EPR's stock price has increased by 14.3%, delivering a total return of 16.2%.

I see that EPR has just shared its results for Q4 2025. At the same time, I understand that the market is concerned with dynamic AI development and increasing adoption. Given EPR's substantial exposure to the theater segment, I've seen that some investors are concerned if that could be a threat to EPR. I will explore this later, but I would like to discuss EPR's latest results in the first place.

EPR Remains My Key Holding In The REIT Portfolio

EPR in a nutshell

I consider EPR a very unique REIT in the market as it focuses on experiential properties. Its portfolio was historically oriented around theaters (and they still represent a substantial share of adj. EBITDA - 36%), but I see that EPR also invests in eat & play venues, fitness centers, ski resorts, waterparks, etc.

With a $100B total addressable market, EPR has a lot of opportunities to choose from, and that's why EPR has managed to keep its theater portfolio at a high quality.

What's important in EPR's business model? The structure of its leases. I'd like to highlight that EPR is a triple net lease REIT. I consider a triple net lease to be a highly favorable type of lease that involves the tenant in covering a substantial part of costs regarding the property (operations, maintenance, taxes, insurance, repairs, etc.).

That's why I think EPR's contractual rent escalators (generally 1.5%-2%) are so effective. They go nearly straight to the AFFO, positively impacting EPR's growth and shareholder returns. I think that's especially attractive given EPR's relatively long WALT of 11 years and well-balanced lease expiration schedule.

The portfolio is becoming better diversified, but theater exposure is still there

As of Q4 2025, EPR had 333 properties, but I see that theaters still represent 148 of them. At the same time, they account for 36% of adj. EBITDAre, so I understand that some investors are still concerned with a high exposure to this segment.

But I'd like to say that I'm satisfied with EPR's efforts on portfolio reorganization over the last couple of years. On one hand, EPR kept disposing of the least effective (most often vacant) theaters, as I heard the management comment during the last Earnings Call:

In the past 5 years, we have sold 33 theaters. We had one remaining vacant theater. Disposition proceeds totaled $168.3 million in 2025.

At the same time, I'm very happy to see that EPR expects a much higher investment volume in 2026. It suggests to me that interest rate drops are finally starting to bring some effect into the market. I think the expectation gap between buyers and sellers (in terms of valuation) is decreasing, and that's why EPR can expect larger investment volume:

Our disciplined deployment strategy has enabled us to expand the depth and breadth of our portfolio of experiential properties over the past several years. Our investment spending throughout 2025 and heading into 2026 reflects our deep relationships and high-quality opportunities. We are announcing investment spending guidance for funds to be deployed in 2026 in the range of $400 million to $500 million.

Some investors are concerned with AI

I follow Jussi Askola, and I very much enjoy reading his articles. I think he shares valuable insights and has great expertise in the REIT sector. I hope he won't hold this against me, but I have to disagree with him about EPR. Here's Jussi's Sell rating on EPR I've read recently, which I'm going to refer to.

I encourage you to read it as it presents a different perspective from mine, but in short - he argues that a dynamic development of AI-facilitated tools will hurt theaters. He assumes that people will be less likely to visit theaters as large blockbuster movies will be easier to create for platforms such as Netflix (NFLX). But I don't think that will be the case.

It's about experience; AI-generated slop was and never will be enough

I can find AI-generated music on YouTube or basically anywhere on the Internet. If I can do that, other people can do this as well. Despite that, I haven't stopped going to concerts, live music events, etc. and I know that many people still do that. I don't think that people are going to simply accept AI slop and AI-generated content as content of equal value and quality to "real" materials. It's about experience.

Culture requires soul, and with the increasing problem of too many low-quality shows storming streaming platforms, people will be more about quality than quantity

Music, films, books. All these sources are a part of a cultural landscape. Culture requires soul behind it. I don't think anything can have the same magic when it's AI-generated. I will always prefer to read Dostoevsky over an AI-generated text trying to resemble his work. I have the same feelings about movies, music, or any other content.

It was never just about the movie itself; the theater is a place to spend quality time with your loved ones

I don't think that going to the theater is not just about watching a movie. It's about the experience of a big screen, shared time outside with friends and family, and a much higher quality of the whole thing. I can't imagine watching Dune 2 just in my own home; it would be missing out from my perspective.

If that was the case, why should we bother with doing anything offline?

I have a similar reasoning behind gaming REITs such as VICI Properties (VICI) and Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI). Why should people bother going to these casinos with so many online platforms available? Once again, it's more than just gaming, watching a movie, reading a book, or listening to music. It's about the experience, which I hope will never lose its meaning to us as a human race. I don't think AI-generated content will ever replace "real" music, movies, books, or other forms of culture, art, and entertainment.

Either Way, I'm Not Buying Anymore

Yes, I'm not buying EPR anymore. Don't get me wrong, I'm not selling any of my shares either. EPR is a Hold for me. I see that EPR's stock price has increased by over 20% YTD, which I appreciate, but I'm not eager to invest at these prices.

While I have a positive outlook on EPR in general, I like to remind myself that it's not a risk-free investment. Concentration is never a positive aspect of any business. I feel comfortable about EPR's theater exposure, but it still constitutes a risk factor if we experience another COVID, if some theater operators struggle, or, who knows, maybe Jussi is onto something and AI will turn out to be a major threat (contrary to my opinions and even hopes).

I'm aware that EPR lies on a riskier side of the REIT sector; that's why I prefer to hold onto my shares instead of buying more at an 11.2x forward-looking P/FFO multiple.

Wall Street analysts seem to share my modest outlook on EPR, and their consensus it a slight downside amounting to nearly 3%. I'm pasting their expectations below for you to have a reference point.

Ok, so with all that being said, I'd like to highlight one more reason why I believe EPR is worthy of a place in my and your portfolio. I want to hold it, because it has a strong income potential. Its current dividend yield amounts to 6.2%, but I've managed to secure a larger average yield-on-cost.

What I find even more reassuring is that EPR has recently announced a dividend increase of 5%, which I see as a very positive news, especially given its bumpy recovery post-COVID.

To summarize briefly, I don't intend to sell any of my shares, but I prefer not to buy anymore. EPR is a classic example of a good REIT to hold, collect dividends, and reinvest elsewhere.

data.title: JEPI: Higher Payouts Ahead
data.description:
It has been an interesting start to 2026. There has been back-and-forth on tariffs that have introduced volatility. We have seen an AI-disruption fear sweep the market and knock several sectors back that could, in theory, be displaced by AI, adding volatility. We have also had a volatile start to March here with the Iranian strikes and the new conflict that has been going on this week in Iran. It has been a lot to digest for the markets. But in uncertain times, income and cash flow for your portfolio are paramount. And today, we are coming back to recommend one of our favorite monthly income-generating names, the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), to see how it’s holding up in this volatile 2026 environment.

While the broader market has been choppy, JEPI has been holding up well this year, rising over 3% year-to-date, not counting dividends paid. While JEPI took a bit of a knock to start the week, we think the higher volatility can help boost some of the returns with JEPI via higher distributions. And those distributions are what you are after, as this instrument is specifically designed to provide high monthly cash flow. For an income-focused investor, a sideways start to the year when you are still collecting a 7% to 8% yield is actually a solid win, more so since the principal has been holding up. One thing we always caution is that when buying high-yield instruments, there is the risk of principal erosion, where you basically trade your paper value for cash distributions. However, JEPI is holding its ground quite well, which is a testament to its design and a diversified internal structure where top holdings are less than 2% each. If your goal is to keep your capital intact while collecting a steady paycheck, seeing the fund trade sideways is actually a validation of the strategy. It’s not a growth engine, but it is a reliable income generator.

So how does the fund operate? Well, it is not a simple covered call strategy, but it utilizes a unique 80/20 split. While the bulk is in traditional equities, about 20% is funneled into equity-linked notes (or ELNs), which generate income via options. Because of this structure, when volatility spikes, the payout potential actually rises because option premiums get much juicier. While interest rates and shifting market conditions mean the monthly dividend is not a fixed, consistent number, the fact that JEPI has paid out over $26 in distributions since inception shows it is doing its job well. Sure, market trends do matter, as we saw distributions trend lower during the lower-volatility days of 2022-2023, but the 2025-2026 environment has provided enough choppiness to keep the income stream attractive for those who can be patient. We like it. Understanding and digesting all of the holdings of this ETF is beyond the scope of this column, and the portfolio is always in flux, but below are the most recent holdings (as of 3/3/2026):
