So, as you can see in the top common holdings, we have dividend income there (you can scroll on the table to look at the additional columns). The max port value currently is Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) at 1.75% of the portfolio. The equity values will rise and fall week to week based on the market and the sector. But in addition to the common stock dividends, where the income can move is the ELNs. Here is the deal, folks: the volatility of late is great for the portion of the portfolio in the ELNs. You see, since JEPI is essentially selling these options (via the ELNs), it collects more money when volatility is high. This is why you’ll often see JEPI’s monthly dividend spike during months when the market is swinging wildly, as the fear in the market is literally being converted into cash. As such, we expect some higher payouts are in the cards thanks to recent activity, likely with the next announced distribution. The most recently announced distribution was this week, $0.3513 for the month, and was paid today (the time of this writing). Since February was a bit more volatile, this was higher than the $0.3444 the month prior. We suspect April will be higher so long as this volatility continues. But of course, the distribution does fluctuate, so market conditions will dictate moves in the payout.

Make no mistake, the market for two years, outside of a few smaller corrections and the March and April 2025 selloff from tariffs, volatility has been low. What do you notice above in 2025? Payouts spiked in the spring of 2025 due to more volatility. Well, we have been volatile, so that is why higher payouts are in the cards. For the long-term investor, the math is actually pretty compelling. If you do some simple math on the current yield, you could theoretically recoup your entire initial principal investment in about 13 years just through these monthly distributions alone, assuming the yield holds firm. Our approach at BAD BEAT Investing is to blend these kinds of income plays with growth and value positions, often converting short-term double-digit profits into lifetime house positions, while income-generating machines like JEPI provide added cash flow for more investment. We still like the name as a hold right now, but the best way to play it is either through consistent monthly dollar-cost averaging or by waiting for those sharp market dips to build your position at a lower cost basis. While capital appreciation is a bonus, and we have seen a steady climb for about a year, we view this as a buy and hold for the cash flow.

data.title: DGS: The Purest Emerging Markets Small-Cap Value ETF
data.description:
Intro

From a fund strategy standpoint, ETF.com had a great summary of The WisdomTree Emerging Markets SmallCap Dividend Fund ETF (DGS): It tracks a dividend-weighted index of small-cap, dividend-paying emerging market stocks. It holds emerging market companies that have paid dividends over the past calendar year and weights them based on the size of dividends paid. DGS only includes stocks that are in the bottom 10% of the total market cap of the WisdomTree Emerging Markets Dividend Index. The resulting portfolio is a proxy for an Emerging Market Small Cap Value asset class (EM SCV).

You typically see EM SCV language pop up when building a factor-based portfolio that "tilts" to a certain asset class. In a previous article, I explained why this may be a good idea. The performance speaks for itself.

But in this article, I want to focus on the performance of DGS compared to other similar funds. While it has actually underperformed compared to peers, I actually think it's still a great time to buy simply because of the concentrated factor loadings in the small-cap space. To me, DGS is a better option for the small cap value emerging market ("EM SCV") space. Although total risk-adjusted returns, expenses, and turnover have lagged competitor funds in recent years, factor regression statistics are still in favor for DGS.

DGS vs Peers

Some funds to compare DGS to would be the Avantis Emerging Markets Equity ETF (AVEM) and the Avantis Emerging Markets Value ETF (AVES). I'll get into the key differences later in the article, but these are some well-respected emerging market funds that are used to target emerging markets in a globally diversified portfolio. Performance, however, has dragged for DGS the last 3 years, with DGS at a 48.16% total return versus 60.72% for AVES and 70.38% for AVEM.

And from a risk standpoint, DGS is a bit more concentrated in number of holdings, due to its tighter strategy, but the least concentrated in top 10 overweight. Turnover is a bit high, and holdings for DGS are pretty diversified across several sectors as well.

From a fund summary standpoint, DGS is a bit older than the others with a slightly higher expense ratio due to its strategy. It also is slightly more liquid than AVES, but the most popular fund is clearly AVEM, however.

Overall, this comparison does not put DGS in favor. It has underperformed, tax inefficient turnover, and is more expensive. There is one thing DGS has going for it however, and that is the factor statistics revealing it's a true EM SCV fund.

Regression

If one is specifically trying to target EM SCV, perhaps for a barbell approach with large caps, DGS is the superior choice due to its favorable size factor loading. While the other similar funds have outperformed, they are technically in different ballgames.

For example, DGS size loading ("SMB") is -0.22 where AVES is -0.32 and AVEM -0.41. While on the surface, this doesn't seem like much, the orders of magnitude relative to each other are drastic. Taking a ratio of these means DGS size loading is around 31% smaller than AVES and 47% smaller than AVEM. In other words, DGS is comprised of much smaller companies, whereas the other could be more categorized as mid-cap, at least by factor loadings. DGS and AVES value loadings ("HML") are also twice as good as AVEM. Comparatively, DGS is a true EM SCV fund, AVES is more of a EM MCV fund, and AVEM is just a EM fund. So if one is to target the EM SCV asset classes, DGS is a winner, with AVES a close second.

Conclusion

AVES has outperformed DGS on every metric, so why would we even entertain DGS?

The answer lies in its factor loadings and a mean regression of the small-cap premium. DGS offers one of the purest exposures to the emerging markets small-cap value factor currently available. While AVES and AVEM have delivered stronger recent returns, their factor exposures suggest they sit higher in the market cap spectrum. For investors specifically targeting EM small-cap value, DGS remains the closest proxy. Factor investing is about maintaining exposure to compensated risk factors over long horizons and not chasing recent winners. If the small-cap premium in emerging markets reasserts itself, funds with deeper exposure to that factor, like DGS, should be positioned to benefit the most. I am long DGS in my personal globally diversified factor-tilted portfolio with a 10% weight and rebalancing.

Additional Considerations

Though this is more of a comparison article, I wanted to briefly touch on the state of the EM SCV state as a whole and why someone might overweight this asset class even after the 30-40% run-up.

From 1995-2026 EM delivered a 6.43% CAGR on a 0.29 Sharpe ratio due to very large volatility swings. Over the same timeframe, the US stock market delivered a 10.88% return on a 0.59 Sharpe. In other words, EM has underperformed on a return basis and nearly half the risk-adjusted return to the US market. Believers in highly efficient markets are watering at the mouth here since, under such conditions, all sound asset classes should deliver similar risk-adjusted returns over the long run. This means either US stocks must underperform to match EM or EM must vastly outperform. We've seen something similar from 1994 to 2007, and mean reversion is looking juicy here.

I'm not the only one who thinks this as well. AQR recently published expected returns for different market listings, with EM outperforming the US by a magnitude of nearly 20%.

Although I think DGS is better to target the small cap space, which evidence has shown is due to a mean reversion, any of the listed funds would be a great addition to a globally diversified portfolio.

data.title: OAKM: Actively Managed Value ETF Following A Questionable Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In a Christmas Eve interview on CNBC's "Squawk On The Street", Bill Nygren, portfolio manager of the Oakmark U.S. Large Cap ETF (OAKM), noted the challenge active managers faced in 2025, where, unless they were overweight the mega-cap growth stocks, it was difficult to keep up with passive indices. Nygren also noted the unusual strength of the momentum factor that year and indicated his belief that some form of mean reversion will occur this year. With OAKM, Nygren has put his money where his mouth is by selecting stocks with significantly weak price momentum, and while that strategy could work well, it introduces shareholders to significant earnings variability and concentration risks. As a result, I'm uneasy about OAKM's composition, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

OAKM Overview

According to its website, investors should consider OAKM for three reasons:

1. Access to an experienced large-cap value investment team led by Bill Nygren, Michael Nicolas, and Robert Bierig.

2. Consistent investment philosophy that identifies quality companies priced at a discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

3. Active management in an exchange-traded fund structure that provides transparency, intraday liquidity, and the potential for tax-efficient investing.

Estimates of intrinsic value depend on the manager, so that doesn't really tell us much. However, I wanted to see how the team defined "quality", and I found that in the fund's prospectus, the advisor looks for companies with solid free cash flows and earnings that are growing and reasonably predictable. In addition, the advisor looks for a "high level of company management alignment with shareholders," which, based on the description below, relates to stock ownership percentages and performance-based compensation.

Unfortunately, the prospectus makes no mention of traditional quality metrics (e.g., margins, capital efficiency ratios) and only outlines management's general "bottom-up" approach to stock selection. Therefore, I'll assess OAKM's quality independently later in this article compared to its two benchmarks, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD) and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

Lastly, key fund characteristics for OAKM include a 0.59% expense ratio, $1.08 billion in assets under management, and a December 3, 2024, inception date. That means OAKM's history is short and likely not too meaningful, but the team still has a good long-term track record of outperforming the S&P 500 Index with the Oakmark Fund (OAKMX). As shown below, despite struggling over the last few years, OAKMX has outperformed by 2% per year since its inception in August 1991.

OAKM Performance Analysis

From January 2025 through February 2026, OAKM has gained an annualized 17.96%, compared to 20.27% and 15.60% for IWD and SPY, respectively. You can see in the chart below that OAKM jumped out to an early lead but saw its growth flatten in mid-2025 and has more or less matched SPY's returns in the first two months of 2026. Meanwhile, IWD is up 7.22% YTD, clearly a major benefactor of the market's rotation to value.

One likely explanation is that OAKM is highly concentrated in the Financials sector, with exposure currently sitting around 33%, down from about 38% in January 2025. As a proxy, consider that the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) is down 6.10% YTD, so at least in the short term, the fund faces this sector headwind. However, its above-average exposure to Energy (14%) is proving beneficial after the U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran. Crude oil futures are up to $74 per barrel at the time of writing, or about 29% YTD.

Earnings Variability Risk

Regardless of your outlook on Financials and Energy , I want to emphasize the earnings variability risk introduced by overweighting these two sectors. Using S&P 500 Index operating earnings per share data for each sector over the short term (last 12 quarters) and the long-term (last 40 quarters), I calculated coefficients of variations, or CVs, by dividing the standard deviation of EPS by the average over the respective period. As shown below, Energy's was by far the highest over the long term (108.6%), while Financials (37.1%) comes in second place behind Technology (42.5%).

I also calculated earnings variability scores for each ETF by weighting their sector allocations by these short- and long-term CVs, and OAKM ranked #135/149 among all the large-cap value ETFs I track. Therefore, you should expect OAKM's returns to be more volatile for the foreseeable future. In uncertain markets, I look to reduce risk, so I view that feature negatively.

OAKM Fundamental Analysis

In my investment thesis, I noted Nygren's belief that the momentum factor would lose some of its strength this year. To a certain extent, I share that sentiment, but I also don't think it's right to buy stocks simply because they're underperforming. Mean reversion isn't somehow programmed into the market, and when I look at OAKM, I see a portfolio that barely beat EPS expectations last quarter (+2.35%). I also see a portfolio that has, on average, somewhere between a "C" and "C+" EPS Revision Score from Seeking Alpha. For context, that ranks #136/149 in its category compared to #66/149 for IWD.

I suggest weak earnings surprises and revisions are good reasons for OAKM's current selections underperforming. As shown above, OAKM's holdings are up only 7.38% and 10.52% on a weighted average basis over the last six months and one year compared to 24.18% and 36.74% for IWD, so I'm not convinced management is exploiting any market inefficiencies. Furthermore, OAKM's quality metrics (EBIT margins and ROE) are below its benchmarks, which I view as a missed opportunity. OAKM managers should be able to improve a passive benchmark's quality features because they can be extremely selective by choosing only 30-40 stocks rather than several hundred.

Extremely Cheap Value: OAKM's True Strength

However, it seems as though management has focused its efforts on finding stocks that trade at extremely cheap valuations. To illustrate, consider that OAKM only trades at 13.42x forward earnings right now, which represents a 27% and 39% discount to IWD and SPY, respectively. The discount is even bigger on a trailing price-to-book and trailing price-to-cash flow, and trying to capitalize on these huge gaps is something that makes sense to me.

It also makes sense if some of its top holdings can deliver on earnings growth expectations. For example, analysts project about 27% EPS growth for Phillips 66 (PSX) and Citigroup Inc. (C) over the next twelve months, which would be a huge improvement compared to their -26.47% and -1.59% annualized growth rates over the last three years. At the portfolio level, OAKM's next-year EPS growth rate is 10.96%, which is excellent from a GARP perspective, given its 13.42x forward P/E ratio. Still, the risk is that much of this expected growth comes from stocks in the Financials and Energy sectors which, as we know from earlier, aren't always reliable. To further illustrate, consider the expected EPS growth rates for these stocks as of March 1, 2025, compared to what they actually delivered over the last year:

Ticker Company Name 1Y EPS Growth Estimate (March 2025) Actual 1Y EPS Growth Difference
COP ConocoPhillips 1.12% -18.69% -19.81%
PSX Phillips 66 -8.29% 116.14% 124.43%
C Citigroup Inc. 32.80% 17.64% -15.16%
KDP Keurig Dr. Pepper Inc. 6.67% 45.71% 39.04%
TRGP Targa Resources Corp. 36.51% 47.91% 11.40%
MRK Merck & Co., Inc. 87.35% 8.01% -79.34%
STT State Street Corporation 12.21% 14.49% 2.29%
DE Deere & Co. -13.42% -21.56% -8.14%
ICE Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. 10.32% 20.71% 10.39%
AIG American International Group, Inc. 4.47% 62.25% 57.78%

Notice how estimates were wildly inaccurate for Energy and Financial stocks like ConocoPhillips, Phillips 66, and Citigroup, underscoring these sectors' general unpredictability. That's not to say positive results can't happen for OAKM, but I question why so much of the portfolio's value proposition is tied to stocks in these sectors. To me, it seems unnecessarily aggressive, especially given the positioning of other active value funds. The SEI Enhanced Large Cap Value Factor ETF (SEIV) comes to mind. It has a combined 19% in Energy and Financials and also has the edge over OAKM on estimated EPS growth (14.00% vs. 10.96%), forward P/E (11.63x vs. 13.42x), ROE (20.42% vs. 16.27%), and last quarter earnings surprises (7.17% vs. 2.35%). Simply put, I believe the odds of success are greater with SEIV, and it doesn't hurt that its expense ratio is much lower (0.15% vs. 0.59%).

Investment Recommendation

OAKM is an actively managed large-cap value ETF led by three experienced portfolio managers with a solid record of outperforming the market over the long run. Currently, the portfolio is priced cheaply, trading at just 13.42x forward earnings and 8.97x trailing cash flow, and if consensus double-digit earnings growth expectations materialize, buying OAKM today would be an excellent play from a GARP perspective.

However, my view is that OAKM management is taking an unnecessary risk by assigning so much to stocks in the Financials and Energy sectors, whose EPS variability is high and challenging to predict. Furthermore, many of OAKM's selections have negative earnings revisions and weak earnings surprises, and I suggest those as logical reasons for why they've lagged the market over the last year as opposed to any irrational market mispricing. My analysis also revealed only average quality, a disappointing find for a 30-40 stock fund, and from a pure factor perspective, I think there are better options. This article identified SEIV as an alternative, which I invite you to read about here.

Normally, I would assign OAKM a "sell" rating based on its unattractive factor mix, but I've decided to initiate a neutral "hold" instead in recognition of the team's long-term success. After all, it is an active fund, and I expect its managers will pivot if the current weak-momentum strategy doesn't work out. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your questions afterward.

data.title: IPAY: Idiosyncratic Risks And Structural Headwinds (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My first article was written last July, and the subject of that article was the Amplify Digital Payments ETF (IPAY), with the thesis having three main pillars: the structural tailwind of the adoption of digital payments, the valuation of the fund compared to the broader market, and the thesis that the concerns over stablecoins and crypto disruption were overblown.

That call has not aged well. IPAY currently trades at around $45, indicating a decline of approximately 25% from the point at which the original article was written, underperforming the market over that period. The thesis did not adequately factor in the pace and scope of several converging headwinds that have subsequently materialized, ranging from the sharp decline in PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) and Fiserv, Inc. (FISV) fundamentals, to name a few, to a new round of fundamental concern over the threat of AI-driven disruption to traditional payment systems and high interest rates pressuring consumer sentiment.

This follow-up article will review the assumptions, go over what has changed, and give an updated view on the fund.

The Fund

Amplify Digital Payments ETF seeks to replicate the total return of the NASDAQ CTA Global Digital Payments Index, gaining exposure to global traditional companies in the payment space like Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA) and relatively new, disruptive companies in the industry such as crypto giant Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN), providing a balanced approach to gaining exposure to this investment theme.

The index consists of as many as 50 companies with at least $500 million in market cap, at least $1 million in daily traded value over the last three months, and at least a 20% float. The weighting is market cap weighted. You can read up more on the index methodology here.

Since the writing of the initial article, IPAY has dropped substantially in terms of assets under management, dropping to $175 million, and together with the relatively average daily dollar volume traded over the last three-month horizon of $1.85 million, it earns a C Liquidity Grade.

In terms of fund ratings, the fund has just improved in one category- Dividends, going from an F to a C-, mainly because of its track record of 2 years of dividend growth. IPAY has worsened in two fund categories, Momentum and Risk. No surprise here given the performance and volatility it has exhibited the last 6 months.

Briefly on geographical exposure, the fund is U.S.-heavy with around 77% exposure, and the rest goes out to countries such as the Netherlands, the U.K., and Brazil, with 6.16%, 4.77%, and a 3.13% weight, respectively. The bulk of the companies (71%) are large-cap companies above a $10 billion market cap, and around a quarter are mid-cap companies with market caps between $2 billion and $10 billion.

Idiosyncratic Blowups, AI Disruption, and Stablecoins' Integration

Aside from the macro-level disruption and structural headwinds, the fund was hurt by the company-specific stock price collapses of two of its larger holdings, namely PayPal and Fiserv. These were not industry-level disruptions but rather self-inflicted wounds driven by operational issues, lack of earnings guidance credibility, and management changes. Both stocks dropped dramatically, weighing down the fund price, seeing as they were the companies with some of the highest weighting.

PayPal was one of the original article's bright spots, a company that would likely be helped by the inclusion of stablecoins through their PYUSD product and the expansion of NFC technology into Europe. Instead, it was one of the names that hurt the fund the most. In their Q4 2025 earnings report, they missed on revenue and EPS. They drastically missed their guidance for 2026, with guidance to adjusted profit declining or rising marginally, while Wall Street expected around 8% growth.

Fiserv was, if anything, an even more dramatic and unexpected collapse. The original article had cited Fiserv's stablecoin launch as a bullish sign for the industry. Fiserv's stock suffered a 44% drop in October 2025, the worst single-day performance in the company's history. That was the market reaction after the company's Q3 earnings results missed expectations by a wide margin. Management pointed to the slowing growth and margin compression for its Argentina arm, which contributed around 10% of the 16% growth achieved last year. The Clover point-of-sale platform's growth had also halved to 5%, due to increasing competition and customer losses. The Clover POS was a key growth engine. FISV had fallen by 70% in value over the last twelve months and was trading at decade lows by early 2026.

In my original article, I acknowledged stablecoins as a legit risk, but failed to address the potential structural headwind for the payment companies that is the rise of agentic AI.

Last month, a very popular research scenario published by Citrini Research described as speculative in nature a world in which AI agents automatically direct transactions through stablecoin networks rather than card networks, effectively eliminating interchange fees. This scenario had an immediate impact on the markets, with Visa, Mastercard, and American Express dropping significantly, in a single day. Although this scenario is speculative, markets are starting to price in these risks, which were merely theoretical in nature just a year ago.

The rationale behind this concern is simple. The AI agents, entrusted with the power to make transactions on behalf of the consumer, will naturally aim to minimize costs and maximize speed, two areas in which stablecoin rails have an edge over card networks. The interchange fee for card transactions is between 2% and 3.5% per transaction, whereas stablecoin transactions take mere seconds and cost fractions of a cent.

Of course, Visa and Mastercard are not standing still. They have created agentic commerce infrastructure under their "Intelligent Commerce" and "Agent Pay" programs, respectively, which aim to integrate their networks with AI-driven transactions through tokenization and spending capabilities. However, the market is asking for a risk premium for this uncertainty, and it is not yet done.

What Did Not Change

However, some of the original thesis remains intact. The long-term case for digital payments adoption has not disappeared, as global mobile payment market forecasts remain robust, and the transition away from cash continues across emerging markets. The fund's core holdings, such as Visa and Mastercard, retain strong network effects, and their pivot toward value-added services (fraud prevention, cybersecurity, agentic tokenization) provides a credible moat-deepening strategy.

Coinbase, another meaningful IPAY holding, has benefited from the broader crypto regulatory tailwind and continues to gain institutional relevance as stablecoin infrastructure becomes formalized. The GENIUS Act, while a headwind for pure-play card networks, is arguably a tailwind for holdings with direct crypto exposure.

Valuation

From a valuation standpoint, the fund currently trades around a 18.47x P/E, dropping substantially from the 23x from the last time we analyzed the fund. The fund still trades at a steep discount to the broader U.S. market, with it trading at around 27.62x P/E.

The top 10 constituents form around 54% of the fund’s holdings. We can see the general trend of them trading below their 5-year average, which is not unexpected given the risks to their growth in the past period and some idiosyncratic risks we discussed earlier.

Rating Downgrade

The AI-driven transaction rerouting is a more recent and potentially more critical risk than stablecoins. The environment for consumer spending has been more challenged than anticipated. For example, the earnings report from PayPal highlighted issues related to lower- and middle-class consumers, a theme that is being echoed across large retailers. The assumption of normalization of consumer sentiment was part of the original article, and it has taken longer for normalization to happen, with interest rates remaining high, but still expected to drop this year.

The current price level is significantly lower than at the time of the first article, and the factors that led to this decline remain unresolved. Although the long-term story of digital payments looks healthy, the near-term earnings story of some of the largest holdings in IPAY is under pressure.

The revised recommendation is a HOLD recommendation. At current prices, the risk-reward does not seem particularly compelling. Although the discount to the S&P 500 is real, it is justified due to the structural risks.

data.title: Kazatomprom Is Rock-Solid In A High-Return Global Uranium Market
data.description:
The Upward Trend At NATKY Is Fully Justified

The US OTC-listed shares of National Atomic Company Kazatomprom JSC - GDR - 144A (NATKY) are enjoying good momentum, and investors who are rightly positioned long-term in this major global supplier of uranium for nuclear energy are enjoying it too. We will examine positive prospects for their horizon. In our opinion the following is justified: the positive market sentiment along with the majority of Wall Street analysts’ buy signal.

About National Atomic Co. Kazatomprom JSC

Kazatomprom 4Q25 Operations and Trading Update ( 4Q25 Report), issued on 2 February 2026, states:

Kazatomprom is the world's largest producer of uranium with the Company's attributable production representing approximately 21% of global primary uranium production in 2024. The Group benefits from the largest reserve base in the industry and operates, through its subsidiaries, JVs and Associates, 27 deposits grouped into 14 mining assets. All of the Company's mining operations are located in Kazakhstan and extract uranium using ISR technology with a focus on maintaining industry-leading health, safety and environment standards.

Kazatomprom securities are listed on the London Stock Exchange and Astana International Exchange. Kazatomprom is the national atomic company in the Republic of Kazakhstan. The Group's primary customers are operators of nuclear generation capacity, the principal export markets for the Group's products are Asia, Europe, and North America. The Group sells uranium and uranium products under long-term contracts, short-term contracts, as well as in the spot market, directly from its headquarters in Astana, Kazakhstan, and through its Switzerland-based trading subsidiary, TH Kazakatom AG (THK).

For more information, please see the Company website at www.kazatomprom.kz.

The Context: The Long Term Is Promising

At the time of this writing, March 2, 2026, Trading Economics reports the following (“commodity/uranium” section of the website):

Uranium futures in the US were at $88 per pound, trading in a narrow range since pulling back from the two-year high of $101.5 in late January, tracking the drop for industrial commodities as the dollar rebounded and traders eased concerns of dollar debasements. Still, yellowcake contracts remained nearly 10% higher since the start of the year on the bullish view of demand. Recent developments in nuclear power investment have been headlined by operators of power-hungry data centers, driving multiple tech giants in the US to sign contracts for small modular reactors. The US cut regulations on the construction and permits for uranium converters and enrichers and announced deals for the construction of new power plants. These include a partnership with Cameco, which approved the development of Westinghouse reactors, and a fresh $2.7 billion in contracts to Centrus and two other reactors and enrichers to offset the shun of supply from Russia following sanctions on their nuclear fuel.

Other, broader factors than those mentioned by Trading Economics, such as energy security and decarbonization targets, are prompting analysts to predict a considerable jump in uranium demand due to the increasingly rising of nuclear power plants globally.

In fact, the website NuclearNewswire, citing the authoritative source from the World Nuclear Association, wrote:

Demand to double? The World Nuclear Association forecast in a September report that uranium demand for nuclear power is due to rise by 28 percent by 2030 because of energy security and decarbonization goals. The report said that new uranium mines and restarted operations will be needed to meet the growing demand for uranium, and that demand could more than double by 2040 to more than 150,000 metric tons a year, compared with the about 67,000 metric tons in 2024.

Given these prospects, it is obvious that supply can hardly keep pace with the rapidly growing demand for uranium nuclear power plants. According to the law of supply and demand, the price of uranium will inevitably be subject to considerable upward pressure in the long term.

Experts are bullish on uranium prices but warn that emerging markets carry risks and volatility, according to a report from Investing News Network, which also highlights forecasts of $75 to $100 per pound in the short term and $130 to $140 in the long term. This reflects an upward trend from the current price of $86.55 per pound (at the time of writing) as the number of contracts from energy companies will move up.

Market and Industry Robust Context of Kazatomprom

This shift towards robust nuclear power generation will generate sustained demand for uranium and a corresponding price per pound in the long term. In this context, the NATKY Company, a leading global uranium supplier, is likely to benefit. As Kazatomprom highlights in its Q4 2025 Business and Trading Report (4Q25 Report), the company faces a wide range of opportunities to seize amid rising production and contract fulfillment. A trifecta, at least, is providing very positive signs. The first two are boosting demand for reliable uranium suppliers like NATKY, as evidenced by: Kazakhstan’s partnership with the US to deploy SMR technology and training in Central Asia; and Asia’s nuclear expansion, driven by $214 billion in investments from China and India. Capital inflows, such as the Sprott Fund’s multi-billion dollar purchase of ~3,450 tU of physical uranium, could indicate an ongoing trend. Given this favorable context, NATKY naturally needs a free hand, and by coming to the company’s aid, the Kazakh government is surrounding it with a series of regulatory reforms that will further bolster NATKY’s control over uranium resources. NATKY's business will be more resilient to uranium market fluctuations while simultaneously benefiting from a very favorable market environment. If this proves true, we believe investors can expect NATKY to consolidate its strategic position in the uranium sector, which will positively impact profitability.

Kazatomprom – Business Operations and Trading in Q4 2025: The Situation

Due to market conditions, uranium prices were somewhat weaker in 2025, but Kazatomprom's production and Group sales volumes were both very strong, at 9-11% and 11% respectively compared to 2024.

Ahead of the financial results for fiscal year 2025, scheduled to be published on March 20, 2026, Kazatomprom's sales and other key profitability metrics, including free cash flow to dividends, are all advancing.

Outlook for 2026: Operations

Production is expected to reach a superior level at 27,500–29,000 metric tonnes of U₃ O₈ (100% basis).

Sales volume is expected to be 19,500–20,500 metric tonnes.

The financial forecast for 2026 will be published on March 20, 2026, as part of the 2025 annual report.

The Balance Sheet Is Solid: Sound Capital Management Strategy

The most recent available data on Kazatomprom's balance sheet and dividend payout comes from screenshots of some tables from Kazatomprom's interim financial statements for the 3 and 9 months reporting period ending on September 30, 2025.

Higher Cash and Cash Equivalent: Higher 28.7% Year-On-Year:

Kazatomprom's declining long-term borrowings, combined with an intensified use of short-term borrowings, demonstrate, in our opinion, the company's adaptability to the changing global uranium markets. This restructuring presents numerous opportunities; we have already highlighted some positive factors, including a new uranium supply agreement with the Japanese energy provider Kansai Electric Power and the planned production ramp-up at the Budenovskoye joint venture in the Turkestan region of Kazakhstan, as shown in the company’s 4Q25 Report. To capitalize on these opportunities, Kazatomprom needs to supplement its liquidity with short-term loans, plus value potential that could be derived from its inventory. The company needs to concentrate liquidity in the short term.

Back to NATKY’s Sound Capital Management Strategy: This step is strategically important with regard to volatility as well: While the outlook for uranium in nuclear power is bright—with rising demand and robust long-term prices—this also entails significantly greater uncertainties, which can be mitigated by reducing exposure to the long term financial obligations.

Financial Leverage Ratios

  • The Net Debt-to-12-month (LTM) EBITDA ratio is 0.16. Net debt is calculated as follows: “Net Debt = Total Debt - Cash and Cash Equivalents.” It amounts to KZT (“Kazakhstani Tenge”) 169,520 million. Unfortunately, we encounter a problem: Kazatomprom's interim financial statements for the reporting periods up to September 30, 2025, do not show the EBITDA figure. Therefore, we calculated the EBITDA ourselves as follows: 12-month EBITDA = EBITDA Q4 2024 + EBITDA Q1 2025 + EBITDA Q2 2025 + EBITDA Q3 2025. These figures are available on MarketWatch. The total amount is US$2,048.5 million, or KZT 1,047,783.5 million, based on an average exchange rate of approximately KZT 512 per US dollar. Thanks to its enormous profitability, Kazatomprom can pay off its debt in less than two months.
  • Since investors are generally satisfied when the value of this measure is not lower than 1.5, they will assume that NATKY stock, with an LTM Interest Coverage Ratio of 66.06, can easily meet its interest expenses and all other financial obligations arising from outstanding debt.

Note: Aggregate Amount of Operating Income is calculated according to the following formula: Gross Income – (SG&A Expense + Other Operating Expense). The quarterly amounts in US$ for Gross Income, SG&A Expense, and Other Operating Expense, from Q4 2024 to Q3 2025 are available on MarketWatch, and added together. The aggregate amount of Interest Expense is calculated by performing the same procedure on quarterly US$ amounts from Q4 2024 to Q3 2025 available on MarketWatch.

  • The Altman Z-score is 7.37, according to GuruFocus as of this writing. NATKY’s Altman is above 3.0, which implies that the company’s balance sheet is well in safe areas and the risk of insolvency is nil in the coming years.

Dividend Payment

The table in the screenshot illustrates the dividend NATKY has declared in the third quarter of 2025 (in millions of Kazakh tenge):

This screenshot instead illustrates the dividend declared by the company in the third quarter of 2025 but in US$:

NATKY on the OTC Markets

The Global Depositary Receipts (“GDRs”) of National Atomic Company Kazatomprom JSC - GDR - 144A, are listed on the US-OTC under the symbol “NATKY”. These were trading at US$76.43 each at the time of writing, giving it a market capitalization of US$19.8 billion, as the stock has 259.36 million shares or GDRs outstanding. Please note that 1 GDR equates to 1 common share.

Technical Analysis

The stock has a 52-week range of $27.30 to $91.26/GDR, according to Seeking Alpha. The current GDR price is 29% above the 52-week middle point of $59.28/GDR.

The 20-day moving average is above the 50-day moving average. The 50-day moving average is above the 100-day moving average. The 100-day moving average is above the 200-day moving average. This suggests a positive market trend in the short, medium, and long term.

Market sentiment is optimistic, as NATKY is the world's leading supplier of uranium for global nuclear power plant demand, meeting at least 25% of it, implying a significant global market share. Uranium will be needed to fuel nuclear power plants if specifically requested by large tech companies, which will require ever greater energy to power increasingly powerful, faster, and more precise AI-equipped data centers. Furthermore, each country wants to produce more and more energy on its own to become less dependent on others, and decarbonization requires ever greater use of anything that produces zero or very little CO2. At the same time, the economy appears to be focusing on sustainable growth with nuclear energy alongside renewable energies and conventional fossil fuels. From here, the upside potential is considerable, as the RSI at 55.12 is still well below the overbought range.

Valuation

Given the favorable prospects, NATKY Company should continue to pay dividends. The dividend declared in the third quarter of 2025 was 1,264.12 tenge, or US$2.50 per GDR (based on an exchange rate of 505.28 KZT to US$1.00). With quarterly dividend payments, this would result in an annual dividend payment of approximately US$10 per GDR. The dividend yield is 13.1%, compared to 1.14% for the S&P 500. Even with only one payment declared per year, the annual dividend yield would be 3.3%—nearly three times that of the S&P 500 or benchmark for the US stock market.

Based on MarketWatch's figures for key profitability metrics, market capitalization, and our calculation of NATKY's enterprise value of US$20.1 million (= market capitalization plus net debt) at the time of this writing:

  • NATKY's EV / EBITDA (TTM) of 9.81 Vs. Sector Median's 8.09.
  • NATKY's Price / Cash Flow (TTM) of 12.5 Vs. Sector Median's 7.12.

However, based on the same financial metrics, NATKY is significantly cheaper than Cameco Corporation (CCJ), one of the world's largest uranium producers, supporting a buy signal. And concerning the sector median as benchmark, the problem is that a few US-listed uranium producers currently generate stable revenues and positive cash flow from active uranium mining projects. Many others are smaller exploration and development companies and are therefore not yet profitable.

Risks

The financial situation currently poses no risk, as explained in the corresponding paragraph based on the balance sheet strength and relevant debt ratios. Furthermore, NATKY generates positive free cash flow and pays dividends to its shareholders, indicating value creation.

The risk lies in the uranium price, which could fall sharply from here and negatively impact NATKY's profit and growth prospects. This extremely unfavorable scenario would require the uranium price to remain exceptionally low for an extended period. But this is unlikely, as energy demand is rising rapidly due to the AI ​​boom, the defense industry, electrification, decarbonization, energy security, and the need for nuclear power and uranium for power plants. And, NATKY is a global uranium supplier. Should NATKY encounter difficulties, other companies in the global market would have little chance of remaining competitive or surviving. But a negative decline in share prices cannot be ruled out in the event of a uranium price collapse, as nothing can be taken for granted in the financial markets.

The real risk with NATKY lies in the extremely low daily trading volume of its shares on the US OTC market. Seeking Alpha (“Trading Data” section) reports an average volume of 697 shares (over three months) at the time of writing. If an investor wants to sell some shares sharply above the current daily traded volume, circumstances could force him to accept a considerably lower selling price, potentially resulting in a loss.

Conclusion

Shares of National Atomic Company Kazatomprom JSC (GDR 144A, abbreviated NATKY), which trade over-the-counter in the US, are experiencing positive price performance. Long-term investors focused on this leading global supplier of uranium to the nuclear power industry are benefiting from the positive market sentiment and buy recommendations from most Wall Street analysts. The company is expanding and increasing its sales volumes, while the uranium price is well-positioned given the rising demand for this raw material in nuclear power. These investments are necessary to drive economic growth through AI data centers, decarbonization, energy security, and other sectors. Energy-intensive companies are driving these emerging trends.

data.title: Payoneer: Never Happy Despite Hidden Growth
data.description:
Payoneer Global Inc. (PAYO) is back trading at yearly lows despite an insanely cheap valuation. The market is too focused on short-term results impacted by lower interest income, while the payments business has strong long-term prospects. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on the fintech with plans for mid-teens growth.

Never Happy

As covered some in the past, Payoneer Global is a constant grower never given much credit by the stock market. The company reported 2025 revenues of $1.05 billion, up 8% YoY, but far above the levels from when the company went public and ran into problems with the Russian invasion of Ukraine.

As one can see, Payoneer started at $400 million in revenues and has now grown more than 150% in a period when the stock is down substantially. The company grew revenue ex. interest income by 9% in Q4'25 and 14% for the year.

Payoneer reported total payment volumes were up 10% YoY to $24.8 billion. The global payments company has seen strong growth in B2B SMBs and enterprise payouts, and a stablecoin offering in the future will support the cross-border B2B payments business.

The stock is down mainly due to the company reporting lower revenue growth since the U.S. started cutting interest rates, leading to a $25 million reduction in interest income in 2025. The fintech has seen interest income remain flat for 2 years despite a $1.5 billion boost in customer funds.

Payoneer has boosted customer funds to $7.9 billion, up 23% over the 2-year period. With flat interest rates, the company would generate over 10% annual interest income growth versus the forecast for a headwind of up to $42 million this year.

Hidden Growth

The market isn't impressed with the 2026 revenue guidance of ~$1.1 billion, with consensus analyst estimates now forecasting only 6% growth this year to reach $1.12 billion. The adjusted EBITDA will only rise about $10 million above levels going back to 2024.

Payoneer produces the vast majority of profits from the interest income portion of customer funds. The forecast is for the operating business to double adjusted EBITDA this year to ~$90 million, but these improving results in the business only offset the dip in interest income this year.

In addition, the company guided to some ongoing weakness as Payoneer optimizes the customer base to focus on more high-margin customers during 2026. In the Q4'25 earnings release, CFO Bea Ordonez provided the following color around guidance:

In 2026, we expect to deliver $900-940 million in revenue ex. interest and $85-95 million of adjusted EBITDA ex. interest, more than double the prior year. We are taking deliberate actions to optimize our customer portfolio and guidance for revenue ex. interest includes a 300 basis point estimated headwind related to those efforts. We expect to accelerate growth during the year as we execute on our upmarket strategy and lap these headwinds, delivering mid-teens growth exiting the year and beyond, as we continue to unlock leverage in our model from ongoing investments in our platform, including in agentic AI capabilities.”

The stock has now fallen to a market cap of only $1.7 billion. With a cash balance of $416 million, Payoneer only has an EV of $1.3 billion.

The company is forecasting 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $280 million, leaving the stock trading at just 5x adjusted EBITDA. Management is guided to exit the year with a mid-teens growth rate, normally warranting the stock trading at least a similar valuation multiple.

Payoneer still needs to execute on this plan to optimize the customer portfolio and return to 15% payment growth rates. Also, the fintech still faces additional interest rate headwinds in the year, with forecasts for the Fed to cut at least a couple of more times for a total of 50 basis points.

Ultimately, though, the normalized growth rate of the company will generally follow the payments volume growth rates, which were 14% last year. For now, investors face a year where the stock price lags again while the market focuses on actual reported results and not the hidden growth rates.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Payoneer continues to generate strong growth from growing global payments volumes and has plans for catalysts via stablecoins and a U.S. banking charter. Unfortunately, the underlying growth is currently hidden due to lower interest rates. Regardless, the stock should be valued based on normalized adjusted EBITDA growth in the mid-teens range, not just 5x EBITDA.
